content:
# Numbers Chapter 30 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 185–186 | **Source Image:** `NUM_030_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_030_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. pa_0137

---

**1** And Moses spoke to the heads of the tribes of the sons of Israel, saying, "This is the word that YHWH has commanded:

**2** When a man makes a vow to YHWH or swears an oath binding himself under a solemn obligation, he must not break his word. He must do everything that has come out of his mouth.

**3** And when a young woman still living in her father's house makes a vow to YHWH or binds herself under a solemn obligation,

**4** and her father hears her vow or the binding obligation she has placed upon herself, and her father says nothing to her — then all her vows shall stand, and every obligation she has bound upon herself shall stand.

**5** But if her father overrules her on the day he hears of it, none of her vows or binding obligations that she placed upon herself shall stand. And YHWH will release her from guilt, because her father overruled her.

**6** And if she marries a husband while her vows are upon her, or while a rash promise from her lips still binds her,

**7** and her husband hears of it and says nothing to her on the day he learns of it — then her vows shall stand, and the binding obligations she placed upon herself shall stand.

**8** But if on the day her husband hears of it he overrules her, then he nullifies the vow that was upon her and the rash promise from her lips by which she bound herself. And YHWH will release her from guilt.

**9** As for the vow of a widow or a divorced woman — every obligation she has bound upon herself shall stand against her.

**10** And if she made her vow while in her husband's house, or bound herself under a solemn obligation with an oath,

**11** and her husband heard it and said nothing to her — he did not overrule her — then all her vows shall stand, and every binding obligation she placed upon herself shall stand.

**12** But if her husband decisively nullifies them on the day he hears of them, then nothing that came from her lips — whether vows or binding obligations — shall stand. Her husband has nullified them, and YHWH will release her from guilt.

**13** Every vow and every sworn obligation to deny herself — her husband may uphold it, or her husband may nullify it.

**14** But if her husband says absolutely nothing to her from one day to the next, then he has upheld all her vows and all her binding obligations that are upon her. He has upheld them, because he said nothing to her on the day he heard them.

**15** But if he nullifies them some time after he first heard them, then he shall bear her guilt."

**16** These are the statutes that YHWH commanded Moses — between a man and his wife, and between a father and his daughter while she is young, still living in her father's house.

**17** [Note: The Hebrew verse numbering in BHS/Leningrad places verse 17 as 30:17 (= English 30:16). The chapter contains sixteen verses in English versification, corresponding to seventeen verses in the Hebrew. The final verse above (v. 16 in English) is v. 17 in Hebrew.]

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "Heads of the tribes"** (ראשׁי המטות): Moses addresses the tribal leaders specifically, not the entire assembly. This is unusual — most laws in Numbers are given to all the sons of Israel. The reason may be that tribal heads served as judges who would adjudicate disputes about vows, so they needed to know these statutes directly. The LXX (Vaticanus) confirms this reading with ἀρχὰς τῶν φυλῶν.

**Verse 2 — "Must not break his word"** (לא יחל דברו): The Hebrew verb חלל means "to profane, to make common." A vow once spoken is sacred — breaking it does not merely disappoint, it profanes what has been consecrated to God. I rendered this as "must not break his word" to capture the weight of the prohibition dynamically, though the literal sense is closer to "shall not profane his word."

**Verse 2 — "Solemn obligation"** (אסר): The Hebrew אִסָּר refers to a binding obligation, often one of self-denial or abstinence. It is distinct from a נֵדֶר (vow), which is a positive commitment to give or do something. Together, נֵדֶר and אִסָּר cover the full range of religious commitments — promises to do and promises to refrain.

**Verse 5 — "YHWH will release her from guilt"** (ויהוה יסלח לה): The verb סלח is used exclusively of God in the Hebrew Bible — only YHWH forgives. The woman incurs no guilt because the annulment was not her choice. The Vaticanus LXX renders this with κύριος καθαριεῖ αὐτήν ("the Lord will cleanse her"), emphasizing purification rather than forgiveness.

**Verse 6 — "Rash promise from her lips"** (מבטא שׂפתיה): The noun מִבְטָא derives from the root בטא, meaning to speak rashly or without careful thought. This is not a calculated vow but an impulsive utterance — something spoken in the heat of the moment. The law recognizes that such words, once spoken, still carry binding force unless properly annulled.

**Verse 9 — The widow and the divorced woman**: This verse stands as a critical counterpoint to the rest of the chapter. A woman who is no longer under a father's or husband's authority bears full responsibility for her own vows. No one can annul them. This implicitly recognizes her full legal personhood and agency before God — her words bind her exactly as a man's words bind him (v. 2).

**Verse 12 — "Decisively nullifies"** (הפר יפר): The Hebrew uses the infinitive absolute construction (הָפֵר הֵפֵר), which intensifies the verb. The husband's nullification must be complete and unambiguous — not a half-hearted objection. I rendered this as "decisively nullifies" to capture the emphatic force.

**Verse 13 — "To deny herself"** (לענת נפשׁ): Literally "to afflict her soul/self." This phrase appears in connection with fasting and acts of self-denial (cf. Leviticus 16:29, the Day of Atonement). The obligations in view here are not merely ceremonial but involve genuine physical self-denial — fasting, abstinence, and similar disciplines.

**Verse 15 — "He shall bear her guilt"**: This is a remarkable provision. If a husband initially remains silent (thereby upholding the vow) but later nullifies it, the guilt for the broken vow transfers to him. His silence constituted ratification; his later reversal creates the violation — and he, not she, bears its consequences. This is one of the clearest examples in Torah of transferred moral liability.

**Versification note**: The Hebrew text (BHS/Leningrad) counts Numbers 30 as having 17 verses, with what English Bibles number as 29:40 being 30:1 in Hebrew. The English versification follows the chapter division where 30:1 in English corresponds to 30:2 in Hebrew. My translation follows the English verse numbering (16 verses for chapter 30), which aligns with the content division where verse 1 begins Moses' address to the tribal heads. The Vaticanus LXX similarly treats this as a self-contained unit beginning with Moses' speech.
